title: Hamas attacks Israel; the Gaza war begins
summary: At about 06:30 on the morning of Saturday 7 October 2023, a Jewish holiday and the fiftieth anniversary of the outbreak of the 1973 Yom Kippur war, Hamas and other Palestinian armed factions in the Gaza Strip launched an unprecedented cross-border assault on southern Israel. Under cover of a rocket barrage of some 5,000 projectiles, several thousand fighters breached the Israeli perimeter fence at more than thirty points and attacked communities across the Gaza envelope, including the Nova electronic music festival at Re'im, kibbutzim such as Be'eri, Nir Oz and Kfar Aza, and the town of Sderot. According to Israeli figures 1,195 people were killed on and around 7 October, including some 815 civilians, and 251 people were taken hostage into Gaza. It was the deadliest day for Jews since the Holocaust. Israel declared war the following day. The war Israel launched in response has, as of mid-2026, killed more than 80,000 Palestinians in Gaza according to the Gaza Health Ministry, displaced almost the entire population of 2.3 million at least once, and reduced much of the strip's built environment to ruin. It has also opened a wider regional conflict pulling in Lebanon, Iran and Yemen, and triggered legal actions against Israel at the International Court of Justice and the International Criminal Court.

sections.heading: Background

image.caption: Benjamin Netanyahu, Israel's longest-serving prime minister, returned to office in December 2022 at the head of the most right-wing coalition in Israeli history.
image.credit: Avi Ohayon / Government Press Office of Israel

sections.body:
Hamas, or Harakat al-Muqawama al-Islamiya, was founded in Gaza in December 1987 during the first Palestinian intifada as an offshoot of the Muslim Brotherhood and dedicated to armed resistance against Israel. It won the last Palestinian legislative elections held, in January 2006, and after a brief and violent split with Fatah forces in June 2007 took full control of the Gaza Strip. In response, Israel and Egypt imposed a comprehensive land, sea and air blockade of the territory that remained in force in October 2023. Between 2008 and 2021 Israel and Hamas fought four major rounds of fighting - Operation Cast Lead (2008-9), Operation Pillar of Defense (2012), Operation Protective Edge (2014) and the eleven-day May 2021 war - along with numerous smaller escalations. Each ended in a ceasefire and a return to the status quo of blockade and rocket fire.

By 2023 the Israeli-Palestinian conflict had drifted low on the international agenda. The Trump-brokered Abraham Accords of 2020 had established diplomatic relations between Israel and the United Arab Emirates, Bahrain, Morocco and Sudan, all without any concession to Palestinian statehood, and the Biden administration was pushing for a similar breakthrough between Israel and Saudi Arabia. Inside Israel, Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, back in office since December 2022 at the head of a coalition that included the far-right ministers Itamar Ben-Gvir and Bezalel Smotrich, was consumed by his government's controversial judicial-reform legislation, which had produced the largest sustained protest movement in Israeli history through 2023. In the West Bank, settlement expansion, settler violence and Israeli army raids into Jenin and Nablus had produced the deadliest year for Palestinians there since 2005.

In Gaza itself, Hamas's military wing, the Izz ad-Din al-Qassam Brigades, had been quietly preparing for the operation for years, under the code name 'Al-Aqsa Flood'. Its planners, led by the head of Hamas in Gaza, Yahya Sinwar, and the military chief Mohammed Deif, exploited Israeli complacency about the fence, the technical Israeli surveillance grid, and the reduced state of alert on a Sabbath and holy day. Egyptian intelligence had reportedly warned Israel days before that 'something big' was coming from Gaza. Signals-intelligence analysts in Israel's own Unit 8200 had flagged the possibility of a mass Hamas breakout, based on a captured Hamas planning document known internally as 'Jericho Wall', but the assessment did not reach the highest levels.

sections.heading: What happened

image.caption: The car park near Re'im where some 260 people were killed at the Nova music festival on 7 October 2023, photographed on 12 October.
image.credit: Kobi Gideon / Government Press Office of Israel

sections.body:
The attack began at 06:29 local time with a rocket barrage of unprecedented intensity from Gaza. Within minutes, Hamas fighters using bulldozers, motorised paragliders, boats, and explosives breached the border fence at more than thirty points along its length. Several thousand men, most from Hamas's Nukhba special-forces unit but also from Palestinian Islamic Jihad and other factions, poured into Israel in the largest infiltration in the state's history. Israeli military bases along the border, including the Re'im base of the Gaza Division, were assaulted in the first hours; the divisional headquarters was overrun. Israeli response forces were slow to arrive; in many kibbutzim of the Gaza envelope, small local security teams held out for hours, sometimes a full day, before regular military relief.

The Nova electronic-music festival, held near Re'im and attended by some 3,500 mostly young Israeli and international revellers, was among the first sites attacked. Some 364 civilians were killed there, according to Israeli authorities, in what became the single deadliest event of the day; dozens more were taken hostage. In the surrounding kibbutzim the killings were house-to-house. In Kibbutz Be'eri, home to some 1,200 people, more than 130 residents were killed. In Kibbutz Nir Oz, roughly a quarter of the population was killed or taken hostage. In the town of Sderot, Hamas fighters fought a running battle with Israeli police at the local police station, which was eventually destroyed by an Israeli tank. Reports and later investigations documented widespread killings of civilians in their homes and of festival-goers, the burning of houses, and, in a number of cases confirmed by Israeli, UN and other independent investigations, sexual violence against women and girls. By the end of the day the Israeli death toll stood at over one thousand, with a final revised figure of 1,195 including 815 civilians and 380 soldiers and police, and 251 people, including small children, elderly civilians, and foreign nationals, taken hostage into Gaza.

The Israeli response began that same day and hardened rapidly. On 8 October Netanyahu formally declared war under Article 40 of Israel's Basic Law, the first such declaration since 1973. Defence Minister Yoav Gallant announced on 9 October a 'complete siege' of Gaza: 'no electricity, no food, no fuel, everything is closed'. Israeli airstrikes hit hundreds of targets a day across the strip. On 13 October the Israeli military ordered the roughly 1.1 million residents of northern Gaza to move south within twenty-four hours, in what became the largest single displacement of the war. Ground operations began on 27 October. By the end of December much of Gaza City and Jabalia had been destroyed and Israeli forces had reached Khan Younis, in the south. A one-week ceasefire from 24 November secured the release of 105 hostages in exchange for 240 Palestinian prisoners.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences

image.caption: Destruction from Israeli airstrikes in the Gaza Strip, October 2023, photographed by a Gaza City resident.

sections.body:
The war has extended, at this writing in mid-2026, well beyond the October 2023 opening. The Israeli campaign moved into Rafah, the last Gaza population centre it had not entered, in May 2024, over the public objections of the United States. Palestinian dead recorded by the Gaza Health Ministry passed 40,000 in August 2024, 45,000 by early 2025, and 80,000 by early 2026; independent scholars including a January 2025 letter in The Lancet argued the true toll, when unrecorded and indirect deaths are counted, was very likely higher. The UN and international aid agencies have described most or all of Gaza's 2.3 million people as internally displaced, often multiple times, and warned repeatedly of famine conditions in the north.

On 29 December 2023 South Africa filed a case at the International Court of Justice under the 1948 Genocide Convention, alleging that Israel was committing genocide in Gaza. In provisional-measures orders on 26 January and 24 May 2024 the ICJ ordered Israel, among other measures, to prevent acts of genocide, to allow humanitarian access, and to halt its Rafah offensive. Israel has denied the genocide charge and has continued military operations. On 21 November 2024 the International Criminal Court issued arrest warrants for Prime Minister Netanyahu and former defence minister Gallant, and for the Hamas military chief Mohammed Deif, on charges of war crimes and crimes against humanity, including the use of starvation of civilians as a method of warfare. Yahya Sinwar was killed in a chance encounter with an Israeli infantry patrol in Rafah on 16 October 2024; Mohammed Deif was killed by an Israeli airstrike on Khan Younis on 13 July 2024; the Hamas political-bureau chief Ismail Haniyeh was assassinated in Tehran on 31 July 2024, an operation widely attributed to Israel.

The war rapidly widened. Hezbollah, the Iranian-backed Lebanese movement, opened a supporting front on the Israeli-Lebanese border on 8 October 2023 that escalated through 2024 into a full Israeli air-and-ground campaign that killed the movement's long-time leader Hassan Nasrallah in a Beirut airstrike on 27 September 2024. Iran and Israel struck each other's territory directly for the first time in April and October 2024, and again in June 2026 in the so-called Twelve-Day War. The Houthis in Yemen launched missiles and drones at Israel and at commercial shipping in the Red Sea, forcing much international container traffic to divert around the Cape of Good Hope. Ceasefires and hostage releases in January-March 2025 and again in October 2025 have paused but not ended the fighting.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The events beginning on 7 October 2023 have already reordered the politics of the Middle East and the wider world. Inside Israel the failure of the intelligence services, the army and the political leadership to prevent the attack, and the abandonment of the border communities in the first hours, provoked a searching public reckoning that has not yet run its course. A state commission of inquiry was still, in 2026, months away from its final findings. The Netanyahu government's determination to continue the war until 'total victory' against Hamas has produced deep splits with the families of the remaining hostages and with a substantial part of Israel's own society. Israel's relationship with the United States, its indispensable ally, has come under greater strain than at any time since the presidency of George H.W. Bush.

For Palestinians the consequences have been catastrophic. The Israeli campaign has destroyed hospitals, universities, whole neighbourhoods and much of Gaza's cultural heritage. According to UN OCHA figures cited in early 2026 more than sixty percent of the strip's housing stock has been damaged or destroyed; UNRWA schools, mosques and churches sheltering displaced people have been repeatedly struck. Restrictions on food, medicine, fuel and shelter aid have produced conditions the UN Special Rapporteur on the right to food, in a December 2024 report, called an 'engineered famine'. The West Bank, though not the site of the ground war, has seen its own escalation of Israeli raids, settler violence, and the death of more than a thousand Palestinians since October 2023.

Beyond the immediate parties, 7 October and the war that followed have transformed the international politics of the conflict. South Africa's ICJ case, joined by Ireland, Spain, Colombia, Bolivia, Mexico and others, has recast the conflict in the language of international humanitarian and criminal law rather than diplomatic negotiation. Recognition of a Palestinian state by Ireland, Norway and Spain in May 2024, and by additional European states thereafter, has broken a long Western taboo. In the streets of London, New York, Berlin, Cape Town, Jakarta and hundreds of other cities, sustained mass protests have made Gaza one of the defining moral questions of the mid-2020s.

figures.name: Benjamin Netanyahu
figures.role: Prime Minister of Israel; declared war on 8 October 2023 and has directed the Gaza campaign since
figures.credit: Avi Ohayon / Government Press Office of Israel, CC BY-SA 3.0

figures.name: Yahya Sinwar
figures.role: Head of Hamas in Gaza and principal architect of the 7 October attack; killed by an Israeli patrol in Rafah on 16 October 2024

figures.name: Mohammed Deif
figures.role: Longtime commander of the Izz ad-Din al-Qassam Brigades and co-planner of the 7 October operation; killed by an Israeli airstrike in Khan Younis on 13 July 2024

figures.name: Ismail Haniyeh
figures.role: Hamas political-bureau chief and chief external negotiator; assassinated in a Tehran guesthouse on 31 July 2024 in an attack attributed to Israel

figures.name: Yoav Gallant
figures.role: Israeli defence minister at the outbreak of the war; announced the 'complete siege' of Gaza on 9 October 2023 and was later named in ICC arrest warrants alongside Netanyahu

images.caption: The site of the Nova music festival massacre near Re'im, photographed five days after the 7 October 2023 attack.
images.credit: Kobi Gideon / Government Press Office of Israel

images.caption: Destruction in the Gaza Strip after Israeli airstrikes, photographed in October 2023.
